title: Measure the room with sound
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Build a tiny digital tape measure with the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor, upload the Arduino sketch, and watch distance readings appear in Serial Monitor.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove diagram showing HC-SR04 VCC connected to 5V, Trig to GPIO 13, Echo to GPIO 14, and GND to ground on the ESP32-S3 GPIO extension board.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A small development board that runs the learner's code and controls or reads physical pins.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: A helper board that spreads ESP32-S3 pins into easier-to-reach rows.

parts.name: HC-SR04 ultrasonic ranging module
parts.explanation: A distance sensor that sends an inaudible sound pulse and listens for the echo.

parts.name: Female-to-male jumper wires
parts.explanation: Removable wires with a socket on one end and pin on the other for solder-free connections.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: The desktop app used to open, compile, upload, and monitor the Arduino sketch.

parts.name: Official sketch
parts.explanation: The Freenove Arduino program for Project 19.1 Ultrasonic Ranging.

wiring.reason: Powers the ultrasonic module in the official Freenove circuit.
ESP32-S3 measures the returning echo pulse.
Board and sensor need the same electrical reference point.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical step at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
Offer short explanations on request; do not dump theory before the learner has a working circuit.
If the learner is stuck, check wiring, board/port, baud rate, and USB cable before changing code.
Preserve a working baseline before suggesting modifications.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Place the HC-SR04 so the transducers face outward.
Wire VCC to 5V, Trig to GPIO 13, Echo to GPIO 14, and GND to GND.
Compare wiring to the official diagram before powering/uploading.
Move a flat object 10–30 cm in front of the sensor and observe readings.

arduino.explanation: Gives GPIO 13 the readable nickname trigPin.
Gives GPIO 14 the readable nickname echoPin.
Measures how long the echo pin stays HIGH.
Converts echo travel time into one-way distance in centimeters.

micropython.explanation: Configures GPIO 13 as the trigger output.
Configures GPIO 14 as the echo input.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Send a ping, wait for the echo, measure the time, and convert time into distance.
theoryModel.formula: distance = speed of sound × echo time ÷ 2
theoryModel.whyDivideByTwo: The sound travels to the object and back, but the learner wants only the one-way distance.
theoryModel.whyReadingsWobble: Object shape, angle, softness, movement, and air conditions can change the echo. Small wobble is normal.

test.successCriteria: Readings appear in Serial Monitor and change when the target moves.

firstChecks: stay at least a few cm away

challenge: Measure three targets, record expected and actual distances, then choose a keep-out-zone threshold and explain why.
logbookPrompts: What would you build if the sensor could warn you when something gets too close?
